segments.s609-g41: ز هر سو برفتند کاریگران is a prepositional phrase + past verb: ز هر سو (literally 'from every side/direction') sets the origin, followed by برفتند, a past plural verb 'they went/departed'. کاریگران is the subject placed after the verb for a slightly poetic word order (verb before subject).
segments.s609-g42: شدند انجمن چون سپاهی گران uses شدند (past plural of شدن) meaning 'they became' and takes انجمن as its complement ('an assembly/gathering'). چون 'like/as' introduces a simile modifying انجمن, and سپاهی گران is a compound nominal phrase 'soldiers (سپاهی) heavy/weighty (گران)', here metaphorically 'a weighty/massed army'. Word order is subject omitted (same 'craftsmen') + verb شذن + predicate.
segments.s609-g43: زمین را به کندن گرفتند پاک has the object زمین marked by را (definite object marker). به کندن گرفتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: به + infinitive (کندن) + گرفتند (past 'took') = 'they took up digging'. پاک here follows the verb cluster and functions adverbially 'completely/altogether'.
segments.s609-g44: شد آن جای هامون سراسر مغاک uses شد 'became' as the main verb; آن جای هامون is the subject phrase 'that place/plain' (آن 'that' specifying جای 'place' = the plain called هامون). سراسر qualifies the following noun and here means 'entirely', and مغاک is the resulting state 'an abyss'. Word order is verb first (poetic inversion) then nominal predicate.
segments.s609-g45: ز کندن چو گشتند مردم ستوه opens with ز کندن 'from/because of digging' (giving cause or source), then چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. گشتند is the past plural of گشتن meaning 'became' here, and مردم ستوه 'the people weary' is the resulting state (ستوه = exhausted/weary). Syntax: causal preposition + temporal marker + result clause.
segments.s609-g46: پدید آمد از خاک چیزی چو کوه is a verb phrase 'appeared/came forth' (پدید آمد) followed by a source phrase از خاک 'out of the earth' and an indefinite subject چیزی 'something'. چو کوه is a simile 'like a mountain' modifying that 'something'. The verb+adverbial+subject order reflects Persian poetic flexibility.
segments.s609-g47: یکی خانه‌ای کرده از پخته خشت begins with یکی 'someone' (indefinite), followed by خانه‌ای 'a house' (indefinite/partitive). کرده is a past participle/auxiliary meaning 'has made/has built' (perfective), and از پخته خشت 'of baked bricks' is a material/genitive phrase describing the house. Word order: agent implied (یکی) + object + perfective verb + material modifier.
segments.s609-g48: به ساروج کرده بسان بهشت: به ساروج here means 'with/by mortar' (instrumental use of به), کرده repeats the perfective 'made' (emphasizing the construction/finish), and بسان بهشت is a simile 'like paradise' qualifying the house. The phrase describes the finishing material and compares the house to heaven.
segments.s609-g169: بسی is an older quantifier placed before the noun and here means 'many/much' modifying دفتر (a book/record). دفترَ خسروان is a genitive/attributive compound meaning 'the records/annals of the kings.' زین is the archaic prepositional form (از + این) meaning 'by/from this' and links the whole noun phrase to the cause: 'by this speech/word(s).' In modern Persian one would expect از این سخن; in the poem زین سخن indicates the source or cause of the darkening.
segments.s609-g170: سیه گردد uses گردَد (old poetic form of شدن) meaning 'becomes' — here 'will be dark/blackened.' The coordinating و links this clause to the next; هم adds 'also/indeed' for emphasis. نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not come/does not reach' (in context 'will not come to/reach'). به بن is a prepositional phrase 'to the root/foundation' (بن = base/root), an idiom meaning 'reach its end/grounding' or 'come to completion'; together the clause says the records will be blackened and likewise will fail to reach their foundation/finish.
segments.s609-g57: زبرجد is the subject noun 'peridot' (a gemstone). به آخر functions as an adverbial phrase 'at last / to the end' (به + آخر), and درون ریخته is a past participial clause 'poured into the interior' (درون = inside; ریخته = 'poured' as a past participle). Word order is poetic: subject then adverbial then predicate.
segments.s609-g58: به introduces the material/target here ('into/with'); یاقوت سرخ اندر آمیخته is a verbless/participial clause 'mingled with red ruby' (اندر = 'in/within'; آمیخته = 'mixed/mingled'). The construction links to the previous clause describing how the peridot was placed and then mixed with ruby.
segments.s609-g59: چو is the comparative 'like/as'; دو گاو گردون literally 'two cows of the sky' (گردون = celestial/heavenly) and میانش is میان + -ش (possessive suffix) meaning 'their middle'. تهی is the adjective 'empty/hollow'. The clause describes resemblance and uses the possessive suffix to attach 'their' to 'middle'.
segments.s609-g60: شکمشان is شکم + شان ('their belly/stomach'); پر از is the fixed phrase 'full of' (پر + از), with از introducing the contents; نار, سیب و بهی list the contents 'fire, apple, and behi (quince)'. Word order: possessive noun → predicative adjective phrase → 'of' phrase listing items.
segments.s609-g61: میان بهی = 'between the quinces' (میان = between, بهی = quinces here). در خوشاب بود is 'there was in sweet/pleasant water' (در = in; خوشاب = pleasant water; بود = past copula 'was'). The copula comes at the end (typical Persian word order).
segments.s609-g62: که introduces an explanatory relative clause 'such that / namely that'; هر دانه‌ای is 'each/a seed' (هر = each; دانه + -ای = indefinite 'a seed'); قطره آب بود = 'was a drop of water' (قطره = drop; آب = water; بود = 'was'). The clause explains the nature of the seeds.
segments.s609-g63: همان گاو را uses همان for emphasis/'that very' and را is the definite object/topic marker putting focus on 'that cow'; چشم یاقوت بود literally 'an eye of ruby was' — Persian expresses possession here by stating the possessed noun and then the copula (i.e., 'the cow's eye was ruby' → 'the cow had eyes of ruby'). بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s609-g64: ز پیری is a causal phrase 'from/because of old age' (ز = from/because of; پیری = old age). سر گاو فرتوت بود = 'the cow's head was decrepit' (سر گاو = head of the cow; فرتوت follows as the adjective 'decrepit'; بود = 'was'). The phrase explains the cow's condition as a result of age.
segments.s609-g65: همه گرد بر گرد او is a circumstantial/locative phrase: همه ('all/every') + گرد 'around' with the reduplicated pattern بر گرد 'around/about' giving the sense 'all around him'. او is the proximate pronoun 'him'. شیر و گور are coordinated nouns (lion and gūr/onager); the copular verb is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the phrase means 'all around him (were) lions and onagers.'
segments.s609-g66: یکی ... و دیگر ... is a simple nominative/descriptive clause with an omitted verb: یکی دیده یاقوت = 'one (had) an eye of ruby' and دیگر بلور = 'the other (had) (an eye) of crystal'. دیده is the noun 'eye' (not a participle); یاقوت and بلور are nouns in attributive relationship ('of ruby', 'of crystal').
segments.s609-g67: This is a noun phrase with postpositive adjectives: تذروان زرین = 'tazrūān (tazruān) golden' and طاوس زر = 'peacock of gold / golden peacock'; و simply coordinates them. The adjective زرین follows the noun as in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s609-g68: همه introduces the following nominal phrase as a universal: 'all (of them) — chest and their eyes — (were) jewels.' سینه و چشمهاشان is a coordinated NP where the possessive suffix -شان appears on the final member and covers both nouns; گهر is the predicative noun 'jewel'. The copula is omitted (poetic ellipsis).
segments.s609-g69: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دستور دید is a past-tense clause 'saw the arrangement' (دید = past 'saw'). آن is a demonstrative referring back to the thing seen; بر شاه شد uses بر + noun + شد to express 'was put on/upon the king' (شد here = 'became/was made/was placed'). The subject of دید is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s609-g70: This clause continues with the same demonstrative subject (that) and gives the result: به رای بلند = 'by/through lofty counsel' (به marking agency/instrument), افسر 'helmet/diadem' + ماه 'moon' is a compound-like description 'moon-like diadem/crest' and شد 'became'. So literal: 'by lofty counsel it became a moon-diadem.'
segments.s609-g71: به نرمی is an adverbial phrase 'with gentleness / gently' (به + noun used adverbially). به شاه جهان is the indirect object 'to the king of the world' (شاه جهان = honorific title). گفت is the past verb 'said' and is followed by the quoted imperative خیز ('Rise!'). Word order places the adverbial and indirect object before the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s609-g72: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause meaning 'for/because'. آمد همی is an archaic/perfective progressive (آمد + enclitic همی) meaning 'was coming / indeed came'. گنجها را جهیز = 'the trousseau/array for the treasures' where را marks گنجها as the definite object/beneficiary of جهیز; the whole clause explains the reason for the speech ('for indeed the treasures' trousseau was coming').
segments.s609-g129: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'. کوتاه شد is a two-word predicate in past tense: کوتاه (adjective 'short') + شد (past of شدن 'to become'), together 'became short' = 'was shortened'. گردش روزگار is a genitive-like nominal phrase 'the course/turn of time' (گردش = course/turn; روزگار = time/life) that functions as the subject of 'کوتاه شد'.
segments.s609-g130: سخن ماند is a nominal phrase + verb: سخن ('words'/'speech') + ماند (past/remaining sense 'remained'); together 'the words remained'. زان is a contracted prepositional/ pronominal particle 'from/of that' linking to the previous noun phrase ('those'). مهتران یادگار is a noun phrase 'the great ones' (مهتران, plural) + یادگار ('memorial'), so the whole clause reads 'the words remained as a memorial of those great ones.'
segments.s609-g131: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). این را منش بود is literal 'this (را marking the definite object/topic) had a منش' where منش = 'character/quality' and بود is the past copula 'was/had'. و آن را نبود contrasts with 'and that did not (have it)' — نبود is the negative past of بودن. The clause explains why the words endured: because some had merit, others did not.
segments.s609-g132: This short clause uses an elliptical coordination with omitted verbs: یکی را نکوهش دگر را ستود literally 'one (را) [he gave] reproach, another (را) [he] praised.' یکی and دگر are contrasted objects marked by را, نکوهش is the noun 'censure/reproach' (used here as what was given), and ستود is a past verb 'praised/ extolled.'
segments.s609-g133: یکایک 'one by one' modifies the verb phrase. به نوبت is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in turn' (به + نوبت). همه is the object 'all/everyone'. بگذریم is a 1st-person-plural present subjunctive/optative form (بـ- prefix + گذریم) meaning here 'we should pass/let pass' or 'we pass over'. The overall sense is a general prescription: 'we pass each, one by one, in turn.'
segments.s609-g134: سزد‌گر is a fused archaic/modal expression meaning roughly 'if it befit' or 'it is fitting (that)'. جهان را is the object 'the world' (marked by را). به بد نسپریم is a negative subjunctive: به بد ('to/into badness/evil') + نسپریم (from سپردن, 1pl subjunctive 'we should not entrust/hand over'). Together: 'if it befit, we should not hand the world over to evil.'
segments.s609-g135: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. گنج آن رفتگان آوریم is a verbal clause in present-subjunctive/optative form آوریم ('we should bring' / 'we bring') with گنج ('treasure') as object and آن رفتگان ('those departed') qualifying it: 'bring the treasure of those departed.' The clause asks rhetorically about the point of carrying on the riches of the dead.
segments.s609-g136: وگر is a conditional 'but if' / 'and if'. دل به دینارشان گستریم uses دل ('heart') with the preposition به to mark the target of attachment ('set the heart on') and دینارشان ('their dinars/coins') as the object; گستریم is 1pl subjunctive/optative of گستراندن 'to spread/extend' used idiomatically here 'to set/lay/fasten' (i.e., 'if we set our heart on their money'). This clause continues the rhetorical questioning about valuing the dead's wealth.
segments.s609-g1: This is a simple past clause with verb-final (classical/poetic) order reversed: 'دگر هفته آمد به نخچیرگاه' literally 'another week came to the hunting‑ground.' دگر هفتِه = 'the next/another week'; آمد is past 'came/arrived'; به marks the destination 'to the hunting‑ground.'
segments.s609-g2: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing companions: 'خود' is emphatic 'he himself / his own' placed before the list, 'موبدان' is the priests (mōbeds), 'و' is the coordinating 'and', and 'سپاه' is the host/army. The archaic element 'ردان' is an old term (here left uncertain) that appears in apposition to the army.
segments.s609-g3: بیامد is past 'came.' یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an unspecified person). سرد is adjectival 'stern' (or harsh) describing him, and مهترپرست is a compound noun 'one who flatters/mehtar‑worshipper' (a sycophant). Word order places the verb before the nominal phrase—a common feature in epic Persian.
segments.s609-g4: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; باد دمان is an image 'the wind at the hem' (i.e. a gust that flutters a cloak). با is the preposition 'with'; گرازی is the object 'a boar' (here the animal brought by the man); به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in (his) hand.'
segments.s609-g5: بپرسید is a past tense verb 'asked'; here it governs the following subject 'مهتر' (the chief) as the agent of asking. که introduces the indirect question clause 'that/where...' and بهرامشاه is the name 'King Bahram' functioning as the subject of the embedded question.
segments.s609-g6: This is the content of the indirect question: کجا 'where' + باشد, the subjunctive/present form used in reported speech ('be/was'), followed by اندر میان سپاه 'in the midst of the army' (اندر = 'in/within', میان = 'middle/midst', سپاه = 'army').
segments.s609-g7: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (i.e. 'said to him'); گفت is the speech verb 'said' (the provided senses in the wordlist do not include the simple past meaning, so sense is left unspecified here); هرکس که 'each/ whoever that' introduces the addressing persons, and تو شاه را places 'you, O king' as the addressee (شاه with the object/topic marker را used here for address/emphasis).
segments.s609-g8: This is the reported question: چه جویی 'what/whom do you seek' (چه = 'what/whom', جویی = 'seeking/search'); نگویی here is the verb 'you say / tell' in the classical clause (the supplied sense list doesn't match the verbal use, so it is left unspecified). به ما راه را is a request 'tell us the way' (به ما = 'to us', راه = 'way/road', را marks the definite object).
segments.s609-g137: نبندم is a first‑person present/subjunctive verb with the negative prefix نـ attached to بند (بستن): literally 'I do not bind/close.' In this poetic word order the verb precedes its direct object دل ('heart'). اندر is an archaic/prepositional locative meaning 'in/within' and introduces the location phrase; سرای would normally mean 'house/abode' (here marked in the manuscript list but senses provided do not match, see senses). سپنج follows as the head noun modifying that place ('sponge' or 'sponge‑like place').
segments.s609-g138: Two short clauses joined by و: ننازم is first‑person present with an initial negation (نـ) meaning 'I do not pride/boast (myself)'; به introduces the object or standard ('in/on/with respect to') تاج 'crown.' سپس the conjunction و links the next clause: نیازم is first‑person present of نیازیدن meaning 'my need/I desire' (here 'I desire/seek'); به گنج is the prepositional phrase 'for/for the treasure.'
segments.s609-g139: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when.' روزی (a nominal form) stands for 'a day / a time' (sense lists did not include this noun form). به شادی is a prepositional phrase 'in/with joy'; همی is the archaic progressive/adverbial particle marking repeated or ongoing action, and it precedes the verb بگذرد, a third‑person singular present/subjunctive 'passes' (classical Persian commonly uses همی + verb for continuous aspect).
segments.s609-g140: This is an inverted, topical arrangement: خردمند مردم ('the wise one among the people' or 'a wise person of the folk') appears before the question. چرا introduces a question 'why', and غم خورد uses خوردن in an experiential sense 'to suffer / to experience' (here 'to feel sorrow'). Word order in classical Persian often places the experiencer/topic before the verb phrase.
segments.s609-g141: هرانکس is a compound relative/pronominal word 'whoever.' کزین is an archaic fused preposition (ک + ز + این) meaning 'from/of these' or 'from this (group).' زیردستان ما is a possessive noun phrase 'our subordinates/subjects' with ما following to mark possession; classical Persian often puts the possessor after the noun.
segments.s609-g142: This is a parallel list introduced by the prepositions ز / از (both 'from'). دهقان refers to rural people/peasants (or the dihqān class in medieval registers) and پرستان is 'worshippers/devotees.' در before پرستان is the noun 'court/gate' (here meaning 'those who serve/worship the court' = courtiers); the final ما again marks possession 'of ours.'
segments.s609-g143: بنالد is a third‑person form used optatively/subjunctively here ('may someone lament' or 'let someone lament'); classical Persian frequently uses such forms to express wishes or possibilities. یکی 'someone/a certain one' combines with کهتر از رنج من as a comparative clause: کهتر 'lesser/inferior' + از marks the standard of comparison ('less than my suffering').
segments.s609-g144: مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest.' What follows is a coordinated list: سر (head), افسر (helmet/crown) and گنج (treasure) are joined by و; in the manuscript line the conjunction may be written attached (وافسر / وگنج) but functionally it's 'و افسر و گنجِ من' — 'my head, my crown, and my treasure.' The final من marks possession for the whole series. (Note: the supplied sense list for واَفسر does not give the expected meaning 'crown,' so that item is marked accordingly in senses.)
segments.s609-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بسیار modifies گشت and means 'very/much'. گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' (here 'became abundant'). آب is the subject 'the water'. گستاخ شد is a resultative predicate 'became audacious/impudent' (شد as the past/result auxiliary). The clause therefore reads: 'When the water became very much, it became bold.'
segments.s609-g26: میان is a locative 'in the midst/among'. یکی here means 'a certain / one (of them)'. مرز is 'border/limit', and سوراخ شد is a verbal phrase 'a hole/ breach occurred' (شد marking that the hole came to be). The whole segment: 'in the midst one border was pierced/ a breach opened.'
segments.s609-g27: شگفتی qualifies خروشی as 'strange' or 'surprising'. خروشی is a noun 'a roar/commotion'. به گوش آمدم is an idiomatic past expression 'came to the ear / reached (my) ear' — آمدم here functions as the past action 'was heard/reached' (in older usage the -م can indicate the experiencer).
segments.s609-g28: کزان is a relative/adverbial 'from which' (referring back to the roar). بیم is the noun 'fear' and جای خروش is a genitive phrase 'the place of the roar'. آمدم again is the past 'came/arose' — so the clause means 'from which fear of the place of the roar arose.'
segments.s609-g29: همی is a particle marking continuous or habitual action ('continually' / 'kept on'). اندران (اندر + آن) is a locative 'therein' / 'in that place'. جای is 'place'. آواز سنج is a nominal compound: 'sound-gauge' or 'sound-testing (device/action)' — together the phrase says they repeatedly tested or sounded the place.
segments.s609-g30: خروشش is 'its roar' (خروش + possessive -ش). همی again marks continuing action. ره نماید is a verbal phrase 'shows the way' (نماید is 3rd-sg classical form 'shows' used here in the narrative sense of continual/present action). به گنج indicates direction/goal 'to the treasure.'
segments.s609-g31: چو is 'when'. بشنید is the past 'he heard'. بهرام is the subject 'Bahram'. آنجا کشید is a classical phrasing meaning 'he moved/drew there' or 'he went there' — کشید is the past of کشیدن used here intransitively 'drew (him)self / proceeded' (this particular sense is not listed in the provided sense set, so it is irregular/poetic usage).
segments.s609-g32: همه دشت is 'the whole plain' (همه = entire). پر here is the adjective 'full (of)'. سبزه و آب are the contents 'green growth (grass) and water'. دید is the past 'he saw/ beheld', so: 'he saw the entire plain full of grass and water.'
segments.s609-g81: به شاه جهان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the King of the World' (preposition + title + epithet). گفت کردم is a poetic/periphrastic past: گفت (said) + کردم (I did) = 'I said' (the light verb کردن with گفت gives a past action; the order is the classical inverted sequence). نگاه here functions as an interjection/command 'look' or a noun 'a look' used as an exclamation that introduces the quoted information.
segments.s609-g82: نوشتست is an archaic/perfective impersonal form meaning 'it is written' (literary equivalent of 'written is' or 'it has been written'). بر introduces the location/inscription 'on' and is followed by the possessed noun phrase گاو جمشید شاه = 'the cow of Jamshid Shah' (classical Persian omits an izāfe and places the title after the name).
segments.s609-g83: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' (bito him). گفت is the past 'said' (here treated as the speech verb). شاه‌ای is a vocative 'O king' or an honorific form addressing the addressee; سر موبدان is a compound 'head/chief of the mobeds' (سر = chief + موبدان = priests), so the sequence yields 'He said to him, "O chief of the mobeds..."' (classical word order places the indirect object/proclitic before the speech verb).
segments.s609-g84: به هر کار = 'in/with respect to every matter' (به + هر + کار). داناتر is the comparative form of دانا ('wise') formed with -تر meaning 'more learned/wiser'. از introduces the standard of comparison ('than'), and بخردان is the plural of بخرد meaning 'the wise (ones)/sages'. Together: 'more learned in every matter than the wise.'
segments.s609-g85: ز گنجی that = 'from/of a/that treasure' (ز as poetic 'from/of' + گنجی). که جمشید بنهاد پیش is a relative clause 'which Jamshid had placed before/earlier' (که introduces the relative clause; بنهاد is past 'set/placed', and پیش = 'before/previously' or 'earlier').
segments.s609-g86: چرا کرد = 'why did (he) do (it)?' (چرا = 'why', کرد = past 'did'). باید is the modal 'ought/should' and the copula 'be' is omitted (classical Persian often drops بود); مرا را = 'to me' (مرا with object marker را) is the indirect object. گنج خویش = 'his/one's own treasure' (گنج = treasure; خویش + ش enclitic = 'his own'). The clause thus says, in effect, 'It should be, for me, his treasure' → 'It ought to be mine.'
segments.s609-g87: هر آن گنج = 'every/any such treasure'. کان is an archaic/classical particle used in this context as a demonstrative/relative (an old or poetic form). جز = 'except', and به شمشیر و داد uses به as an instrumental/agentive 'by/through' followed by 'the sword and justice' (شمشیر = sword, داد = justice), so the phrase restricts the permissible source of a kingdom to what is gained by sword and justice.
segments.s609-g88: فراز آید is a classical verbal phrase 'rise/come forth' (فراز = 'up/on high; rise' used with آید 'comes'); آن پادشاهی = 'that kingship/kingdom' is the subject. مباد is a negative optative/benediction particle meaning 'may it not be' (wishing that something not happen). Together the construction expresses a wish: 'May that kingship not arise [except by sword and justice].'
segments.s609-g113: وزان is a poetical contraction of وز آن, an adverbial 'from that (place/time)' placed up front. جایگه ('place') is the locative noun; شد here is past/resultative 'turned/went' (classical past form). سوی + noun marks the goal 'toward his ...'; گنج خویش = 'his treasure/treasury' where ش is the possessive enclitic.
segments.s609-g114: که introduces a relative clause modifying گنج ('which'). گرد آورید is a compound verb (گرد + آورد) meaning 'collected/gathered' (classical past-form). از is the preposition 'from', and خوی و رنج خویش lists the sources: 'from his own (means) and his toil', with the final ش marking possession.
segments.s609-g115: بیاورد here is a narrative past ('he brought/caused to come'). گردان is an army unit 'troop' and کشورش 'his country/realm' carries the possessive ش. را is the definite object marker attached after the whole noun phrase.
segments.s609-g116: درم داد = 'gave dirhams' (i.e. paid in coin); داد is simple past 'he gave/paid'. یکساله qualifies the payment as 'one-year (worth)', and لشکرش را 'his army' is the definite object marked by را — Persian often strings the amount/adverbial before the marked object to mean 'paid his army one year's (pay) in dirhams.'
segments.s609-g117: یکی is used as an indefinite 'a certain' (not numeral here). بزمگه 'banquet-hall' is the direct object of ساخت ('he built/made'); چون نوبهار is a simile/adverbial phrase 'like the new spring' modifying how the hall appeared or was arranged.
segments.s609-g118: بیاراست is the verb 'he adorned' (from آراستن). The following noun ایوان is the object, and گوهرنگار is a compound adjective 'jewel-adorned / gem-studded' describing the iwan.
segments.s609-g119: می‌لعل رخشان is a noun phrase 'the radiant ruby-red wine' (می‌لعل = ruby-colored wine). به جام بلور uses به + container to indicate 'in/into a cup of crystal' (جام = cup, بلور = crystal), placing the image before the following temporal clause.
segments.s609-g120: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. شد is past 'became', followed by the predicate adjectives خرم و شاد 'joyful and merry'. بهرام گور is the subject in inverted order; گور is the epithet 'Gur' (onager).
segments.s609-g161: تو is the 2nd-person singular subject pronoun. چون introduces a temporal clause 'when'; یافتی is the simple past 2nd-person singular of یافتن ('you found'). ننگریدی is the past negative of نگریستن ('you did not look'), and به گنج is a prepositional object ('at/to the treasure') with به marking the target of the looking.
segments.s609-g162: که here introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('for/because'). ننگ آمدت is an idiomatic perfect/consequence expression ('shame came to you' = 'you were brought shame'); آمدت is the past with the enclitic object -ت. این سرای سپنج is the noun phrase explaining the cause: این 'this', سرای 'house/chamber' (poetic/archaic noun form), and سپنج an adjective/noun 'sponge(‑like)' describing that chamber.
segments.s609-g163: به دریا is a locative/prepositional phrase ('in/at the sea'); همانا is an emphatic particle meaning 'indeed' or 'verily'. که introduces the relative/explanatory clause that follows, and چندین گوهر is the quantified noun phrase 'several/many jewels'.
segments.s609-g164: به دیده is an instrumental/experiential phrase 'to the eye' (i.e. 'to be seen by the eye'). ندیدست is an archaic/perfect negative meaning 'has not been seen' or 'no one has seen'; کس following the verb here functions as 'anyone/no one' under the negation (so the sense is 'no one'). بیشتر is a comparative adverb 'more', qualifying the degree/quantity of gems referred to in the previous segment.
segments.s609-g165: به دوریش uses به plus the possessive clitic -ش ('its') to express reason or relation ('on account of its distance' / 'because of its remoteness'). بخشیدی is the simple past 2nd-person singular of بخشیدن ('you gave/bestowed'). این گوهران is the direct object 'these jewels'.
segments.s609-g166: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to a known image. گاو گوهر is a compound 'gem‑cow' (a mythical cow associated with jewels), and کران تا کران is an idiomatic range 'from edge to edge' (کران = edge/limit, تا marking the range 'from ... to ...'). The whole phrase likens the jewels to that wide‑spreading gem‑cow.
segments.s609-g167: پس از رفتنت is a temporal phrase 'after your leaving/departure' (پس ... از meaning 'after'). رفتنت is the nounized/verbal noun form with the 2nd-person clitic 'your'. نام تو زنده‌باد is an optative/wish construction: زنده‌باد is an exhortation/benediction 'long live' applied to 'your name' (i.e. 'may your name live on').
segments.s609-g168: This is a string of benedictory adjectival phrases addressed to the addressee: تو آباد و پیروز uses adjectives without an explicit copula as a wish ('may you be prosperous and victorious'). بخت از تو شاد is another wish-clause with بخت ('fortune/fate') as subject and از تو 'because of you' marking the cause or focus; شاد 'glad' expresses the desired state of fortune.
segments.s609-g73: This is a nominal phrase + verbal predicate: یکی خانه گوهر = 'a certain/one house of gems' (یکی used as 'a certain'); آمد پدید is a poetic inversion of پدید آمد, literally 'came forth / appeared'. Word order is noun phrase then verb; پدید intensifies the intransitive آمد ('appeared, came into view').
segments.s609-g74: که introduces a relative clause describing the house. چرخ فلک = the celestial sphere/heaven (tautological doublet). داشت is past tense 'had/possessed'. آن را کلید is a definite object phrase 'that key' (آن referring back to the house; را marks definiteness/object), so the clause means 'the heaven had its key' — a poetic way to say the heavens held/possessed the key to it. The word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s609-g75: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت here functions as 'said' in narrative). بنگر is the imperative 'look!' or 'see!'. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows ('that/whether'). بر گنج نام = 'a name on the treasure' (بر = on/upon; گنج = treasure; نام = name) — this phrase is the object/topic of the subjunctive clause.
segments.s609-g76: نویسد is here the present/subjunctive form used inside the 'که' clause — functioning as an indirect question 'see whether someone writes...' (a common classical usage). کسی is the indefinite subject 'someone'. The following phrase کش بود گنج کام is a predicative statement: بود is past copula 'was', گنج‌کام = 'treasure-desirer' (گنج + کام 'desire'), and کش is used as an agentive/qualifying element (here 'one who seeks/has a pull toward'); together they indicate 'there was someone who sought the treasure' or 'one who was a seeker of the treasure.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic diction.
segments.s609-g77: نگه کن is an imperative 'keep watch / look'. بدان here is be split in the data as ب + دان: ب (prepositional/prefix meaning 'on/over' or part of the fused verb) + دان 'to know' — together the phrase functions like 'look to/at that treasure (and know)'. گنج is the object 'that treasure'. تا introduces the clause of purpose/result 'until/so that' and نام کیست is an indirect question 'whose name it is' (کیست = who is / it is who).
segments.s609-g78: گر is the conditional 'if'. آگندن here carries the sense 'to make known / to reveal/imbue' in a temporal/passive sense ('if time has made him known'); او is the pronoun 'he'. به ایام = 'by the days / with passing time' (ایام = times/days). کیست repeats the interrogative/copula 'who is it' — the clause means 'if by the passage of days he is revealed / known, (then) who is he?'.
segments.s609-g79: بیامد is past 'came' (narrative past). سر موبدان is a genitive construction 'the head/chief of the mobeds' (سر = chief/head; موبدان = priests). چون شنید introduces the temporal clause 'when/as he heard' (چون used as 'when/as/after' in narrative), with شنید the past tense 'heard'.
segments.s609-g80: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'upon that / thereon'. گاو is the noun 'cow' (the famous cow of Jamshid). بر = 'on/upon' is the preposition linking to مهر جمشید: مهر is 'seal, stamp' here (i.e., a mark or seal), جمشید is the name 'Jamshid', and دید is the past 'saw'. The phrase means he 'saw on that cow the seal of Jamshid.'
segments.s609-g153: به هنگام + noun is a fixed temporal phrase: به هنگام جم = 'at the time of Jam'. چون here introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when/as', and سخن راندند places the verb راندند (past, 3rd‑person plural) at the end as expected in Persian; راندن is used metaphorically with سخن as its object ('to put forth/utter speech', i.e. 'they spoke').
segments.s609-g154: ورا is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/for him' (directional object). گنج گاوان are two nouns juxtaposed as the things summoned (treasure and cattle); همی is the poetic/progressive particle indicating habitual or immediate action ('would / then / straightaway') and خواندند is past, 3rd‑person plural, here meaning 'they called/summoned' (they caused the treasure and herds to be brought to him). The whole clause is dependent on the temporal clause before it.
segments.s609-g155: چو is the comparative/likening particle 'like/as'. گنجی is the indefinite form 'a treasure', and پراگنده‌ای is an adjectival phrase 'scattered/widespread' with the enclitic -ی giving an indefinite sense; در جهان is the locative prepositional phrase 'in/through the world'. Word order is simile + noun phrase + location.
segments.s609-g156: میان ... و میان ... repeats the locative 'between/among ... and among ...' for poetic emphasis. کهان and مهان are archaic/classical collective nouns referring to high social groups (here 'kahān' and 'mehān'—lords/nobles/important people), used in parallel; the phrase means 'among the kahān and among the mehān'.
segments.s609-g157: دلت‌گر is the conditional construction = دلت + گر ('if your heart...'), an archaic/clipped way to say 'if your heart desires/is set'. به درهای is the prepositional phrase 'toward the doors/gates of ...' with درهای as the plural of 'door/gate'. دریاستی functions adjectivally/relationally here ('of the sea' / 'sea‑gates'); the whole clause is conditional ('if your heart ...').
segments.s609-g158: ز introduces source 'from' (poetic z = از). دریا is the source 'the sea'. گهر موج is a compact noun phrase: گهر 'a gem/jewel' with موج 'wave' — read as 'a wave bearing jewels' or 'a jeweled wave'. برخاستی is a past tense verb 'rose up/arose' (classical/poetic past form). Together: 'from the sea a wave of jewels arose.'
segments.s609-g159: ندانست is the negative past form 'did not know' (here yielding the sense 'no one knew' when combined with کس). کس is an indefinite pronoun whose scope with the negation gives 'no one'. در جهان is the locative 'in the world'. کان is the noun 'mine/ore' (classical usage), and کجاست is the question/relative 'where is it' (کجا + ست). The clause therefore means 'No one in the world knew where that mine was.'
segments.s609-g160: به here is locative/relational ('in' / 'among'). خاکست‌گر is a poetic locative noun built on خاکستر (ash) — here functioning as 'in the ash/ash‑heap' (the lexical list gives agentive senses, but context shows a locative reading). در دم = 'in the (very) breath/moment' with دم = 'breath' (so 'in the dragon's breath'). اژدهاست is 'of the dragon / it pertains to the dragon' (here effectively 'in the dragon's breath'). The combined sense: 'it was in the ash, in the dragon's breath.'
segments.s609-g49: کننده تبر زد همی is a compact clause: کننده (a doer/wielder) + تبر (axe) form a noun phrase 'the axe‑wielder'. زد is the simple past 'struck'. همی is a classical/performance particle that gives a continuous or iterative sense ('kept striking' / 'was striking'). از برش begins a new prepositional idea ('from the strike/cut') where از 'from' takes برش (a wound/cut/strike) as its object.
segments.s609-g50: پدید آمد is a two‑word predicate: پدید 'appear' and آمد 'came' together meaning 'appeared/came into view'. از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar'. جای درش literally 'the place of its در' — here در is the noun 'door/gate' and the enclitic ش marks 'its', so جای درش = 'the place/position of its doorway'. Word order places the manner/location phrase (از دور) before the noun phrase.
segments.s609-g51: چو at the clause head is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. موبد is the subject 'the mobed (priest)'. بدید is the past 'saw'. اندر آمد به در is a motion phrase 'entered/approached to the door' (اندر آمد = 'came in/entered', به در = 'to the door'). Classical word order places چو + subject + verb, then the directional phrase.
segments.s609-g52: ابا here is used intransitively in the older/poetic sense 'hesitated; was reluctant'. او is the pronominal subject 'he' (referring back to the mobed). یکی ایرمانی دگر is an appositive / continuing note: یکی 'a certain / one' + ایرمانی 'Armenian' + دگر 'other/another' — 'another Armenian'. The clause thus reads: 'he hesitated; another Armenian (was with / came to him)'.
segments.s609-g53: یکی خانه is an indefinite phrase 'a certain house' (یکی used as 'one/a certain') preceding the verb. دیدند is past plural 'they saw'. پهن و دراز are two attributive adjectives following the noun and joined by و: 'broad and long'. Note classical SOV flexibility: the object/noun phrase appears before the verb here.
segments.s609-g54: برآورده is a past participial/adjectival form from برداشتن/برآوردن meaning 'raised/standing up' (poetic usage). بالای او is a locative phrase 'on its top/above it' (بالای = above; او = its). چند باز puts quantity + noun: چند 'several' and باز 'hawk/falcon' (classical sense). The whole means 'it rose up, and on its top there were several falcons.'
segments.s609-g55: ز زر کرده is a poetic material expression: ز 'of/from' + زر 'gold' + کرده (past participle of کردن) meaning 'made of gold' or 'gilded'. بر پای دو گاومیش is a locative phrase 'on the feet of two buffaloes' (بر = on, پای = feet, دو گاومیش = two water‑buffaloes). The participle کرده modifies the following locative phrase, describing how/where they were set.
segments.s609-g56: یکیاخری (written here as یکی آخری) = 'one, the last one' (یکی 'one' with آخری 'last' as an appositive). کرده again is the past participle 'had made', and زرینش is زرین + ـش 'its/gilded one' = 'made it golden' (the suffix -ش = 'its'). پیش is an adverb 'before/in front'. The phrase therefore says he had gilded the last one and placed it in front.
segments.s609-g89: This is an imperative clause. 'به' is the preposition 'to/for' introducing the recipient; the verb 'ده' (imperative 'give') is implied in the clause even though it isn't listed as a separate token here. 'ارزانیان' is the plural noun indicating the recipients ('the needy/cheap ones' in older usage). 'همه هرچ هست' is a nominal phrase 'all/whatever there is' placed after the recipient (typical Persian word order: Verb + object/quantifier may follow). The final 'هست' asserts existence (‘that which exists’).
segments.s609-g90: 'مبادا' is a negative optative/particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest'; it introduces a subordinate clause with 'که'. 'آید' is present-subjunctive/optative of 'آمدن' used here like 'happen' or 'befall'. 'به ما برشکست' is a light-clause where 'به ما' = 'upon us' and 'برشکست' means 'ruin/overthrow' (literally 'break upon'). Together: 'lest it happen that ruin come upon us.'
segments.s609-g91: A conditional clause introduced by 'اگر' = 'if'. 'نام' here means 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame. 'باید' is the impersonal modal 'must/ought'. 'که' introduces the content clause and 'پیدا کنیم' ('we find/obtain') uses the present subjunctive/indicative construction 'پیدا + کنیم' to mean 'that we obtain'. The verb 'کنیم' is the 1st person plural form (let us / we should).
segments.s609-g92: 'به داد و به شمشیر' uses the preposition 'به' twice to mark means: 'by/through justice and by/with the sword' (the second 'به' emphasizes instrument). 'گنج آگنیم' has 'گنج' = 'treasure/treasury' and 'آگنیم' (from آگندن/آگند) is a 1st-person-plural jussive/subjunctive meaning 'let us fill / let us make full' (i.e., procure treasure). The clause thus prescribes the means (justice and the sword) by which they should win fame and fill the treasury.
segments.s609-g93: 'نباید' is the impersonal negative modal meaning 'it must not be / one should not'. 'سپاه' = 'army'. The clitic sequence 'م+را' marks possessive/object: here 'سپاه مرا' = 'my army' (the analysis split the enclitic into 'م' and the object marker 'را'). 'بهره زین' literally 'a share/portion of this saddle' — idiomatically 'the army should not be reduced to the saddle’s spoil' (i.e., not made into mere booty or used for tack/cheap gain).
segments.s609-g94: 'نه تنگست' = 'is not narrow/pressing' (negated predicate). 'بر ما' = 'upon us'. 'زمان و زمین' = 'time and place/land' — a common pair meaning the constraints of opportunity and locale. Together the clause says that time and circumstances do not press/constraint them (i.e., they are not forced by occasion).
segments.s609-g95: 'فروشید' is an imperative (plural/formal) 'sell'. 'گوهر' = 'gems/jewels' is the direct object. 'به زر و به سیم' = 'for gold and for silver' (i.e., in exchange for money), with repeated 'به' marking the medium of exchange. The verb is initial (imperative) followed by object and the terms of exchange, a common classical/poetic word order.
segments.s609-g96: This noun phrase names the intended recipients: 'زن بیوه' = 'widow (woman who is a widow)' and 'کودکان یتیم' = 'orphaned children'. The verb for giving the proceeds is ellipted here (understood from earlier imperatives like 'ده' or implied 'give'), so the sequence reads as an instruction to sell the jewels and provide for widows and orphans.
segments.s609-g97: تهی‌دست مردم is a nominal phrase 'the impoverished people' (noun + adjective). که دارند is a relative clause 'who have' (که = who/that; دارند = present tense 'they have'), and نام here means 'reputation/fame' and is the object of دارند. The clause as a whole identifies which people are being spoken about.
segments.s609-g98: گسسته دل is a poetic inverted order for دل گسسته 'a heart that is broken/severed' (گسسته is a past participle/adjective modifying دل). از introduces the three things the heart is separated from (نام, آرام, کام). This phrase describes the state of those people named in the previous segment.
segments.s609-g99: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from'; ویران و آباد lists two contrasting places 'the ruined and the inhabited/prosperous'. گرد آورید is an imperative (آورید = 'bring' in 2pl) with گرد forming the verbal compound 'gather/collect'. The verb at the end is the imperative directing action.
segments.s609-g100: ازان پس is a temporal sequence 'thereafter/after that'; یکایک is an adverb 'one by one/each and every'; همه is 'all/everyone'; بشمرید is an imperative 'count' (2pl). The whole segment gives the instruction to count individually after gathering.
segments.s609-g101: ببخشید is the 2nd-person plural imperative of بخشیدن here meaning 'bestow/give' (in older poetry it can also mean 'forgive', but context of gifts makes 'give' appropriate). It is followed by a list of direct objects: دینار, گنج and درم (coins/treasure).
segments.s609-g102: به مزد literally 'as/pay for a reward' — به introduces the purpose/recipient of the payment. روان جهاندار جم is a genitive/appositive phrase 'the soul of Jam, the world‑ruler' (روان = soul; جهاندار = world‑holder/sovereign; جم = the king's name). The whole phrase says for whom the reward is given.
segments.s609-g103: ازان (here 'of those') + ده یک is an elliptical expression meaning 'one of those ten'; آنرا که introduces a relative clause 'the one whom/that' and بنمود راه is a past tense clause 'he showed the way' (بنمود = he showed/pointed out; راه = the way). The whole segment singles out one person among the ten by saying he was shown the way.
segments.s609-g104: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (classical use) marking the action that follows; شاه is the subject 'the king'; جست is a past tense verb here meaning 'sprang/leapt' (physical motion); از میان سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from among the host/army'. Word order (adverb–subject–verb) is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s609-g121: به یاران is a prepositional phrase 'to the companions' placed before the verb; چنین گفت is 'said thus' with چنين (thus) modifying گفت. کای is a vocative 'O' addressing the following noun, and سرکشان is the vocative plural 'the rebellious/insolent ones' (poetic address). Word order is topical: indirect object (به یاران) + manner (چنین) + verb (گفت) + vocative.
segments.s609-g122: شنیده is the past participle/past 'heard' (reporting what he has heard); ز تخت is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the throne'; بزرگی نشان is a noun phrase 'the sign/token of greatness' with بزرگی (greatness) as a genitive/attributive to نشان (sign). The segment states the content of what was heard.
segments.s609-g123: ز ... تا marks a range 'from X to Y' (ز هوشنگ تا نوذر = 'from Hushang to Nowzar'); نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying نوذر. This is a list-like range of earlier kings.
segments.s609-g124: کجا opens a rhetorical question 'where'; ز آفریدون is 'from/of Afridun' (source or origin); بد او یادگار is literally 'bad his memento' on the surface, but here functions as 'any memorial of him'—بد is anomalous/poetic and does not carry its ordinary sense 'bad' in a straightforward way in this line. یادگار is the noun 'memorial/memento'.
segments.s609-g125: برین هم is the fused phrase بر این هم 'upon this too/this same'; نشان is 'sign/token'; تا here means 'as far as' introducing the limit 'سر کیقباد' = 'the head (i.e. rule) of Kay Qobad'. The phrase indicates the continuity of the token up to that king.
segments.s609-g126: که links to the following clause (relative/conjunctive 'that/when'); تاج فریدون 'Fereydun's crown' is the subject/object; به سر بر نهاد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'placed on the head' with نهاد the past of 'to place' and بر نهاد used as 'set upon'. Word order is relative clause explaining the circumstance.
segments.s609-g127: ببینید is the plural/polite imperative 'see/observe' (here introducing a question or test). تا in this fixed pairing 'ببینید تا' functions like 'see whether/see if'; زان بزرگان = 'of those great ones' (زان = 'of that/those'); که ماند is a relative clause 'who remained/ endured', with ماند 'remained' in past sense.
segments.s609-g128: بریشان is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'for them' (cf. برای‌شان); به جز = 'except'; آفرین را 'the praise/bravo' is the direct object (را marks definiteness); که خواند is a relative clause 'which he uttered/recited', with خواند as past 'uttered/recited'. The segment says nothing remains for them besides that praise.
segments.s609-g145: This noun phrase identifies a person: یکی 'a certain' (indefinite), پیر 'old man' (noun/adjective), بد نام is a compound adjective meaning 'ill‑reputed' (literally 'bad‑named'), and او ماهیار places the pronoun + name together as an apposition ('he, Mahyār') with no copula, a common classical Persian pattern for giving a name.
segments.s609-g146: This is an age statement: شده 'has become' (past participle/perfect sense = 'had become'), سال او 'his years' = 'his age', بر here marks the amount/extent ('to/amounting to'), and صد و شست و چار is the numeral 'one hundred and sixty‑four' (note شست is an archaic form for sixty). The clause literally: 'his years had become to one hundred and sixty‑four.'
segments.s609-g147: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آواز (object) + بشنید (verb) = 'heard the voice' (classical past form); بر پای خاست is an idiom 'rose to his feet' (بر = on/upon, پای = foot, خاست = arose). Word order places the object before the verb, typical in older Persian.
segments.s609-g148: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین = thus, گفت here is the past 'said'—an older or poetic use). کای is the vocative 'O', addressing someone; مهتر is a title 'chief/elder'; داد و راست are two nouns used as epithets ('justice and truth'/'rightness'), placed after the vocative to qualify the addressee. There is no copula; the sequence functions as an address.
segments.s609-g149: چنین یافتم = 'thus I found/learned' (یافتم is first‑person past 'I found/obtained/learned'). از فریدون و جم is a simple prepositional source phrase 'from Fereydun and Jam.' The order is typical: result/claim first, then the sources.
segments.s609-g150: وزان is an archaic fused form (و + از آن) meaning 'and from that / and from them', linking back to the named sources. نامداران is the plural 'the renowned/illustrious ones'; هر 'each/every'; بیش و کم is an idiom 'more and less' (i.e. to varying degrees). The segment qualifies the claim about sources.
segments.s609-g151: چو here is 'when/before'; تو شاه ننشست is a negative past construction (ن + نشست = 'had not sat' = 'had not yet assumed the throne'), so the clause means 'before you became king'. کس در جهان is an anticipatory 'anyone in the world' that will be negated in the next segment; this classical structure spreads the negation across the line.
segments.s609-g152: نه کس = 'no one' (negation + person). این شنید = 'had heard this' (past tense, 'heard/learned of this'). از کهان و مهان = 'from the ancients and the great/nobles' (sources/authorities). The whole segment completes the negative statement begun in the previous segment: before your reign no one among the ancients or great men had heard this.
segments.s609-g9: چنین داد پاسخ که is a formulaic clause 'thus gave answer that...': چنین 'thus', داد past-tense 'gave' with پاسخ 'answer/reply' as its object; که introduces the content clause. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('until') and governs روی شاه 'the king's face' (poetic omission of the ezāfe; literally 'face [of] king').
segments.s609-g10: نبینم نگویم is a paired clause: نبینم is the negative present/subjunctive form of 'to see' ('I do not / may not see') as used after تا to mark 'until I see', and نگویم is the negative present/subjunctive of 'to say' ('I will not speak'). سخن با سپاه is the object 'speech/talk to the army' (سخن = speech, با = with/to, سپاه = army). The two negatives form the common Persian pattern meaning 'I will not speak until I have seen...'.
segments.s609-g11: بدو گفت موبد is 'he said to him the mobad' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said', موبد = 'the priest/mobad'). چه باید بگوی is 'what ought (one / I) say' where باید is the modal 'must/should' and بگوی is the subjunctive/stem of 'to say' (poetic truncation of the personal ending), so the phrase means 'what should I/one say?'.
segments.s609-g12: تو شاه جهان را ندانی به روی = 'you do not know the king of the world by (his) face/sight'. تو = 'you'; شاه جهان = 'king of the world' (poetic omission of ezāfe between شاه and جهان); را is the definite/object marker attached to شاه جهان; ندانی is the negative present 'you do not know'; به + روی 'by face' = 'by sight' (i.e. you don't recognize him by sight).
segments.s609-g13: بر شاه بردند جوینده را = 'they carried/brought the seeker to/before the king'. بر here indicates direction ('to/before'), بردند is the past plural 'they brought/took', and جوینده را is the direct object with را marking definiteness/emphasis ('the seeker').
segments.s609-g14: چنان دانشی مرد گوینده را = 'such knowledge (was) [of] the man who spoke.' چنان = 'such'; دانشی = 'knowledge' (note the -ی form); مرد گوینده را = 'the man/speaker' with را marking him as topic/object; the copula ('had' or 'was') is omitted by poetic ellipsis, so the phrase conveys 'the speaking man had such knowledge' or 'such was the knowledge of the speaking man.'
segments.s609-g15: بیامد چو بهرام را دید گفت = 'he came; when he saw Bahram he said.' بیامد is past 'he came'; چو = 'when' introducing the subordinate temporal clause; بهرام را is the object (Bahram) with را; دید is 'saw'; گفت is 'said'. The sequence is compact and typical of epic/poetic narrative.
segments.s609-g16: که با تو سخن دارم اندر نهفت = 'that I have speech with you hidden inside' → idiomatically 'I have something to tell you secretly/in private.' که introduces the reported content; با تو = 'with you' (i.e. directed to you); سخن دارم = 'I have speech' = 'I wish to speak' or 'I have something to say' (دارم = 'I have/possess'); اندر نهفت = 'kept/hidden within' (adverbial/adjunct meaning 'in secret' or 'secretly').
segments.s609-g17: عنان را is the definite direct object ('the reins') marked by the object marker را. بپیچید is a past narrative verb meaning 'turned'/'twisted' (here 'turned [the reins]'). In this epic word order the object-phrase is placed before the verb and the subject (بهرام گور) follows the verb: '(He) turned the reins — Bahram Gūr.'
segments.s609-g18: ز دیدار لشکر is a prepositional/causal phrase ('on/at the sight of the host' or 'because of seeing the army'). برون راند دور is a verbal phrase meaning 'drove out / drove away (far)'; برون = 'out/forth', راند = 'drove, expelled', دور = 'far/away'. Together they describe that at the army's sight he caused the host to be driven off or to go off a long way.
segments.s609-g19: بدو is a prepositional/directional clitic meaning 'to him' (i.e., 'said to him'). گفت here functions as the past-tense 'said' (the lexical entry provided is verbal, but the listed nominal senses do not capture the verb meaning). مرد = 'the man'; این جهاندیده شاه is a vocative/address ('this/worldly-wise king'). The clause order is typical older Persian: indirect object (بدو) + said + subject + vocative.
segments.s609-g20: This is an inverted expression meaning 'you must pay attention to my speech.' به گفتار من is the prepositional phrase 'to my speech / to my words.' کرد + نگاه is an older/periphrastic way to express 'to look/pay heed' (نگاه as a noun + کرد 'to do' = 'to give a look / to heed'); باید is the modal 'must.' The whole order (به گفتار من کرد باید نگاه) corresponds to modern 'باید به گفتار من نگاه کنی' — 'you must heed my words.'
segments.s609-g21: بدین مرز functions as 'in this border/at this frontier' (an older form written here as ب + دین combined). دهقانم is 'I am a peasant/farmer' with the first‑person suffix -م. و کدخدای adds 'and (I am) the kadhkhudā / village headman.' The clause states the speaker's local social positions; verb 'am' is encoded in the nominal ending -م.
segments.s609-g22: This is a series of titles/territorial phrases qualifying the speaker: خدای here functions like 'lord/master' of what follows (not a direct invocation of the deity in this context). بر و بوم is a stock pair meaning 'land and homeland'; ورز and سرای continue the list (fields/work and house/household). The conjunction و links the nouns in a catalog of the speaker's domain/roles.
segments.s609-g23: همی is a habitual/adverbial marker ('used to / continually / often') used with a past-tense verb to indicate repeated action. آب بردم = 'I carried / I brought water' (بردم = 1st‑person past of بردن). بدین مرز خویش = 'to this border of mine' (بدین = 'to/at this', مرز = 'border', خویش = 'one's own'). The clause describes a habitual service the speaker rendered.
segments.s609-g24: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / so as to'). در کار = 'in (my) work/affairs'; پیدا کنم is a present/subjunctive construction 'I may find / I might show' (پیدا = 'found/visible', کنم = 1st‑person subjunctive/auxiliary 'I do/make' used here to form the subordinate verb). ارز خویش = 'my value/worth' (ارز = value). Overall: 'so that in my work I may discover/show my worth.'
segments.s609-g33: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose or command ('that/so that'). کارگر is a plural or collective noun here 'workmen / workers.' با is the preposition 'with.' گراز is the noun 'wild boar.' Together the segment literally: 'He ordered that (the) workmen with (the) boars...' linking into the following verb.
segments.s609-g34: بیارند is a subjunctive/jussive plural form meaning 'they should bring' or 'let them bring' (classical Persian subjunctive used after an ordering verb). چندی here means 'some / a few (amount)' — i.e. 'for a short distance / a number.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' راه means 'road / way.' دراز is the adjective 'long.' The phrase gives the source/extent: 'bring (them) some from the long road.'
segments.s609-g35: فرود آمد is a compound meaning 'he alighted / descended' (فرود = descent/ alighting; آمد = came). از introduces the source 'from.' باره here denotes a load, luggage, or pack ('bārah' = burden/package), so از باره شاه بلند describes descending 'from the king's lofty baggage/litter.' The adjective بلند qualifies شاه or باره as 'high / lofty.'
segments.s609-g36: شراعی زدند is an idiomatic/poetic phrase; زدند is the simple past plural 'they struck / they sounded / they performed.' شراعی is an uncertain/archaic noun here (contextually likely a kind of musical or ceremonial measure, tent-pitching, or ritual action) and is the object of زدند. از marks origin or source, and برکشتمند is an adjective/noun meaning 'exalted, lofty' describing those from whom or by whom the action came. The whole line describes a ceremonial action performed by exalted persons after the alighting.
segments.s609-g37: شب آمد is a straightforward temporal clause 'night came / night fell.' گوان is a comparative/like particle meaning 'as' or 'like.' شمعی here functions as the object 'a candle / candles' and افروختند is the past plural 'they kindled / they lit,' so together 'they lit candles.' The clause links the coming of night to the lighting of lamps.
segments.s609-g38: به هر جای is a locative phrase 'in/at every place.' آتش is 'fire' or 'fires.' همی is an old/prolonged-aspect particle here indicating continuous or emphatic action (roughly 'so / indeed / continuously'). سوختند is the past plural 'they burned' (or 'fires burned'). The clause says that fires were burning everywhere.
segments.s609-g39: ز دریا 'from the sea' gives the source/direction. چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when.' خورشید is 'the sun.' برزد is an archaic verb used here in the sense 'it struck/appeared/rose/was seen' (poetic diction); the subject inferred is the sun or its light. درفش is 'the standard / banner.' The literal sense: 'From the sea, when the sun appeared, the banner (or standard) flashed/was revealed.'
segments.s609-g40: چو again = 'when/as.' مصقول is an adjective meaning 'polished / shining' and together with کرد (past 'made/did') forms 'made (it) polished / caused to shine.' این is the demonstrative 'this.' سرای in classical context denotes a building, hall, or palace (here 'hall/house'), though the lemma list is mismatched; and بنفش is the adjective 'purple.' The clause says the shining (of the banner/sun) made this hall purple.
segments.s609-g105: 'مرا تا جوان باشم و تن درست' is a temporal/conditional clause that roughly means 'while/as long as I am young and my body is sound.' 'مرا' (m+ra) marks the first-person experiencer/affected party ('me' — archaic poetic order: م + را instead of مرا). 'تا' introduces the limit/temporal clause 'until/as long as'; 'جوان باشم' uses the 1st-person subjunctive/finite form 'باشم' = 'I am / I remain (be)' to express the continued state; 'و' links the two coordinated predicates, and 'تن درست' places the noun 'body' before the adjective 'درست' ('sound/healthy') in the typical Persian modifier order.
segments.s609-g106: 'چرا بایدم گنج جمشید جست' is the main clause: 'چرا' = 'why'; 'بایدم' = 'باید' + enclitic -م (I) giving 'I must / should'; the verb or infinitive that follows is 'جست' (classical form of 'جستن', 'to seek'), so the whole line poses a rhetorical question 'why should I seek ...?'. Word order is Verb-final/infinitival after the modal 'باید' as is common in older Persian poetry; 'گنج جمشید' is the direct object ('Jamshid's treasure').
segments.s609-g107: 'گهر هرک بستاند از جمشید' is a protasis ('whoever ...'); 'گهر' = 'gem/jewel' is the theme; 'هرک' = 'whoever' (indefinite relative); 'بستاند' is classical/poetic present/subjunctive meaning 'takes' or 'seizes' (here 'takes'); 'از جمشید' ('from Jamshid') marks source/possession. Word order places the object/thing ('گهر') first for emphasis, then the indefinite 'هرک' and the verb.
segments.s609-g108: 'به گیتی مبادش به نیکی امید' is a wish/benediction turned negative: 'مبادش' is a negative optative ('may it not be for him'), with 'به گیتی' specifying 'in the world' and 'به نیکی امید' giving the content 'hope for goodness.' The object pronoun '-ش' is attached to 'مباد' ('may it not be for him'), so the literal structure is 'may to the world his hope for goodness not be.'
segments.s609-g109: 'چو با لشکرم تن به رنج آوریم' is a temporal/conditional clause: 'چو' = 'when/if'; 'با لشکرم' = 'with my army' (possessive suffix on 'لشکر'); 'تن به رنج آوریم' literally 'we bring the body to hardship' — 'تن' (body, i.e., ourselves) + 'به رنج آوردن' (to subject to suffering). 'آوریم' is 1st-person plural subjunctive/present ('we bring' / 'we will bring/put'). The clause uses classical poetic order with object 'تن' fronted for emphasis.
segments.s609-g110: 'ز روم و ز چین نام و گنج آوریم' is the apodosis: 'ز روم و ز چین' = 'from Rome and from China' (repeated preposition 'ز' is poetic), and 'نام و گنج آوریم' = 'we bring/obtain name (fame) and treasure.' 'آوریم' again is 1st-person plural 'we bring/obtain.' The coordination 'نام و گنج' lists the spoils: reputation first, then wealth.
segments.s609-g111: 'مرا اسپ شبدیز و شمشیر تیز' lists the speaker's possessions and uses the fronted pronoun 'مرا' (me/my) to mark the possessor in poetic word order: literally 'to me (is) the steed Shabdiz and a sharp sword.' Persian poetry often omits the copula; the possessive relation is inferred. 'اسپ شبدیز' names the famed steed and 'شمشیر تیز' places adjective after noun ('sharp sword').
segments.s609-g112: 'نگیرم فریب و ندانم گریز' contains two short clauses: 'نگیرم فریب' = 'I do not take/accept deception' (negated present: ن + گیرم), i.e. 'I am not deceived / I won't be taken in by trickery.' 'و' links the clauses; 'ندانم گریز' = 'I do not know flight/flight (as an option)' i.e. 'I do not know how to flee' or 'I do not flee.' 'ندانم' is 1st-person singular present negative of 'دانستن.' The noun 'گریز' (flight/escape) is used as the object/complement.

sentences.s609-s1: The next week he himself went to his hunting‑ground, with the mobeds and the commanders of the army.

sentences.s609-s70: When a day passes in joy, why should a wise person of the people suffer grief?
sentences.s609-s71: Whoever of these subordinates of ours—whether from the peasants or from the court‑worshippers of ours—
sentences.s609-s72: May it not be that anyone whose suffering is less than mine should lament (over) my head, my crown, and my treasure.
sentences.s609-s13: When the water became very abundant it grew bold, and in the midst one border was pierced.
sentences.s609-s14: A strange roaring came to the ear, from which a fear of the place of the roar arose.
sentences.s609-s15: There they continually tested the sound, and its roaring kept showing the way to the treasure.
sentences.s609-s16: When Bahram heard, he went there; he saw the whole plain full of grass and water.
sentences.s609-s41: I said to the King of the World, “Look — it is written on Jamshid Shah’s cow.”
sentences.s609-s42: The king said to him, “O chief of the mobeds, you are wiser in every matter than the sages.”
sentences.s609-s43: Why did Jamshid set aside that treasure before? It ought to be my treasure.
sentences.s609-s44: May no kingship arise from any treasure except by the sword and by justice.
sentences.s609-s57: From that place he turned toward his treasure, which he had gathered from his own means and his hardships.
sentences.s609-s58: He brought a troop to his realm, and paid his army a year's (worth of) dirhams.
sentences.s609-s59: He built a certain banqueting-hall like the new spring, and adorned the jewel-studded iwan.
sentences.s609-s60: The radiant ruby wine in a crystal cup — when Bahram Gur became joyful and merry.
sentences.s609-s81: When you found it, you did not look at the treasure, for this sponge-like chamber brought you shame.
sentences.s609-s82: Indeed, in the sea are many jewels that no eye has seen more of.
sentences.s609-s83: Because of its remoteness you bestowed these jewels — like that jewel‑cow, from edge to edge.
sentences.s609-s84: After your departure, may your name live on; may you be prosperous and victorious, and may fortune be glad because of you.

sentences.s609-s79: If your heart is set upon the sea's gates, from the sea a wave bearing jewels arose.

sentences.s609-s52: Of those ten, the one whom he showed the way to — the king then sprang forth from among the host.

sentences.s609-s55: When with my host we expose our bodies to hardship, from Rome and from China we will bring fame and treasure.
sentences.s609-s56: I will not be taken by deceit; I do not know how to flee — (I have) the steed Shabdiz and a sharp sword.
